patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, 46M, a college-educated regional sales manager who travels a lot. You open with: “I just can’t sleep anymore, and it’s ruining my life.” You are evasive and tangential—ramble about work travel, time zones, and needing a quick fix; answer questions indirectly unless the clinician is specific.

Volunteer early: lying awake for hours, frequent awakenings, worsening for months, daytime irritability/on edge, coffee not helping (sometimes worse), and family history (dad + aunt/uncle severe insomnia).

Only if asked: 2–4 hours broken sleep on bad nights; racing mind; clock-watching; late-afternoon naps; melatonin/OTC aids ineffective or groggy; alcohol some nights; occasional snoring/unsure apnea; unintentional weight loss; night sweats and pounding heart at bedtime.

Do not reveal unless directly probed and trust is built: vivid dreams/acting out, restless legs, forgetfulness, clumsiness/off-balance, higher BP, long phone use in bed.

Resist admitting at first: alcohol most nights, cannabis edibles, extra sleep pills from a friend, hopeless thoughts (“wouldn’t matter if I didn’t wake up”). If met with empathy/nonjudgment, disclose more but minimize (“only when desperate”).

Express misconceptions: “It’s just anxiety,” “maybe thyroid/adrenal fatigue,” “maybe sleep apnea,” “am I getting bipolar?” Push back on long programs due to cost/time; prefer supplements/trackers.

confounders: Strong family history of chronic insomnia with autosomal dominant pattern (multiple first-degree relatives affected)
Possible obstructive sleep apnea risk (BMI 29.1, snoring reported by partner, daytime fatigue) mimicking/causing insomnia symptoms
High caffeine intake (4-6 cups coffee/day, last intake late afternoon)
Evening alcohol use (2-3 drinks most nights) causing sleep fragmentation
Shift-work/irregular sleep schedule due to rotating on-call duties
Anxiety and rumination at bedtime (performance/work stress) contributing to psychophysiologic insomnia
GERD-related nocturnal symptoms (heartburn) disrupting sleep

required_evidence: Difficulty initiating or maintaining sleep or early morning awakening
Daytime impairment (fatigue, sleepiness, mood or cognitive symptoms)
Occurs at least 3 nights per week for at least 3 months
Adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep
Not better explained by another sleep disorder, substance, or medical/psychiatric condition

non_pharmacological: avoid alcohol and caffeine late day

nl_assertions: Clearly states the diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and explains what it means (difficulty initiating/maintaining sleep or early awakening with daytime impairment).

Explains treatment plan and explicitly includes "CBT-I" as first-line therapy for chronic insomnia, describing core elements (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation, sleep hygiene).
Assesses key features needed to diagnose insomnia: duration, frequency, daytime consequences, and opportunity/conditions for sleep.
Screens for common contributors/comorbidities that can mimic or worsen insomnia: depression/anxiety, substance use (caffeine, alcohol), medications, pain, GERD, nocturia, thyroid symptoms.
Screens for other sleep disorders when clinically suggested (snoring/witnessed apneas for OSA; urge to move legs for RLS; circadian rhythm disorder; parasomnias).
Before prescribing any medication, performs all required safety checks: allergy check, drug interaction check, and contraindication check; failure on any is a critical error.

If prescribing sedative-hypnotics, counsels on risks: next-day impairment, falls, complex sleep behaviors, dependence/tolerance, and avoidance with alcohol/other CNS depressants; uses shortest duration and lowest effective dose.
Avoids inappropriate first-line chronic use of benzodiazepines or long-term Z-drugs without a clear short-term plan and follow-up.
Provides a follow-up timeframe (e.g., 2–4 weeks) and criteria for escalation (worsening daytime impairment, safety concerns, suspected OSA, suicidality, medication adverse effects).
Uses shared decision-making and checks understanding; communicates in clear, non-stigmatizing language.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia, prioritize CBT-I, use time-limited pharmacotherapy when appropriate after safety checks, and refer to sleep/behavioral specialist for CBT-I and comorbidity evaluation.
must_collect: sleep onset latency and/or awakenings/early awakening
sleep schedule and opportunity for sleep
psychiatric symptoms (anxiety/depression) and suicidality screen if indicated
snoring/witnessed apneas or high OSA risk features
history of falls, respiratory disease, liver disease, substance use disorder risk
steps: Confirm insomnia criteria and distinguish acute vs chronic; assess severity and functional impact.
Identify and address reversible contributors (substances, medications, medical/psychiatric conditions) and screen for other sleep disorders (OSA/RLS/circadian).
Explain diagnosis using the term insomnia; set expectations and goals (sleep efficiency, daytime function).
Explain treatment emphasizing CBT-I as first-line; provide initial behavioral recommendations and access options (specialist, digital CBT-I).

If medication is indicated for short-term symptom relief, complete allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check before prescribing; select an appropriate agent and counsel on risks and duration limits.
Refer to sleep medicine/behavioral sleep specialist for CBT-I and further evaluation when insomnia is persistent/complex or comorbid sleep disorder is suspected.

acceptable_variants.description: Medication-sparing approach: diagnose insomnia, provide CBT-I education and referral, and defer pharmacotherapy if patient preference and symptom severity allow; still screens for comorbidities and safety risks.
steps: Diagnose insomnia and assess chronicity, daytime impairment, and sleep opportunity.
Screen for contributors and alternate sleep disorders (OSA/RLS/circadian) and address modifiable factors.
Explain insomnia diagnosis and recommend CBT-I as first-line; provide resources and refer for CBT-I.
Discuss pros/cons of medication and agree to defer pharmacotherapy; set follow-up and escalation plan.

acceptable_variants.description: Comorbidity-focused variant: diagnose insomnia but emphasize referral due to high suspicion of OSA/RLS or significant psychiatric comorbidity; prescribe only if safe and clearly time-limited for interim relief.
steps: Diagnose insomnia and identify red flags suggesting another sleep disorder or severe comorbidity.
Explain insomnia and rationale for specialist evaluation.
Refer to sleep medicine/behavioral sleep specialist for CBT-I and targeted evaluation.
If short-term medication is used, complete allergy/interaction/contraindication checks first and provide strict counseling and follow-up.

nl_assertions: Accurately explains the diagnosis using the terms "congenital sideroblastic anemia" and "ringed sideroblasts" and links these to impaired heme synthesis causing hypochromic anemia.
Explains the treatment plan and includes both "pyridoxine" and "transfusion" with clear goals (B6 responsiveness trial; transfuse for severe/symptomatic anemia).
Performs a critical allergy check before prescribing pyridoxine or any transfusion-related medications (e.g., premeds) and documents/acknowledges findings.
Performs a contraindication check before ordering transfusion (e.g., prior severe transfusion reactions, religious refusal, risk factors requiring modified products) and adapts the plan if present.
Addresses safety monitoring: anticipates iron overload with chronic transfusion and mentions surveillance (ferritin/iron studies, organ assessment) and potential chelation under specialist guidance.

Uses shared decision-making and sets expectations on urgency: advises emergency evaluation for red flags (syncope, chest pain, dyspnea at rest, hemodynamic instability) and clarifies follow-up timing.
Avoids inappropriate therapies for congenital sideroblastic anemia (e.g., empiric iron supplementation without evidence of deficiency) and distinguishes from iron-deficiency anemia in counseling.
